pages.text: is an energy extensive process and contributes a signiﬁcant portion to the production costs. Also, the temperature rise and decrease during the curing process have to be controlled to avoid tempera- ture gradient and cracking of the concrete products. Curing has a strong inﬂuence on the properties of hardened concrete such as strength, durability, watertightness, abrasion resistance, volume stability, and resistance to freezing and thawing and deicer salts. It is reported that the energy consumption is 2300 kJ for each nor- mal weight standard concrete block and 2500 kJ for each standard lightweight concrete block [16]. In an earlier study, it was found that the CO 2 consumption was very low when pressure-compacted cement or mortars specimens were exposed to CO 2 immediately after molding [14] although most cementitious materials such as Portland cement, granulated blast furnace slag, steel slag, and coal ﬂy ash can react with CO 2 [15]. Many factors affect the reactions between CO 2 and cement clinker minerals [17–19]. This study investigated the effects of pre-conditioning on the CO 2 curing of lightweight concrete block mixtures. 2. Experimentation 2.1. Raw materials ASTM 150 Type III Portland cement was used in this project. The chemical com- position and physical properties of the cement are given in Tables 1 and 2 . The ex- panded shale lightweight aggregate (LWA) used in this project was block mixture with loose unit weight of 945 kg/m 3 (59.0 pcf) and a moisture content of 17.0%. Sieve analysis of the LWA is given in Table 3 . Its grading meets both ASTM C330 and ASTM C331 [20,21]. A locally available natural siliceous ﬁne sand with a speciﬁc gravity of 2.65 was used. Its sieve analysis is shown in Fig. 1.C O2 gas with a concen- tration of 99.5% was used for CO 2 curing of concrete in this study. 2.2. Preparation of specimens and testing programs 2.2.1. Mixture proportion The concrete mixture proportions used in this project were designed based on the production of lightweight loadbearing concrete block as speciﬁed in ASTM C90 [22]. A total of eight mixtures (A1–A4, B1–B4), as summarized in Table 4, were de- signed and tested during the preliminary laboratory study. The mixture proportions in Table 4 were based on the mass of wet LWA containing 17.0% moisture (satu- rated surface dry) and sand containing 4.5% respectively. Additional water was Table 1 Chemical compositions of ASTM Type III Portland cement. added for both compaction and optimum CO 2 curing purposes. The effective W/C in Table 4 refers to the mass ratio of total water (including both water in aggregates and additionally added water) to cement. 2.2.2. Preparation of specimens It is a general practice that lightweight aggregates are mixed with water before adding the cementitious materials due to their porous structure and high water absorption. The mixing procedure used in the lab was as follows: (1) mixed light- weight aggregate with sand ﬁrst for 30 s, (2) added all required additional water and mixed for another 30 s, and (3) added all cement and mixed for 2 min. The concrete mixtures were ﬁlled into and compacted in a steel cylinder mold with an internal diameter of 10 cm. Uniformly mixed concrete mixtures were added into the steel mold in three layers with approximately equal volume, slightly compact each layer, then compacted using a compression machine until the specimen reached 10 cm in height and held for additional 3 min. The fresh density of specimens was controlled at around 1840 kg/m 3 to ensure not exceed- ing the maximum oven dry density of 1680 kg/m 3 as speciﬁed for lightweight concrete. Compacted specimens were then placed in different pre-conditioning environ- ments, as summarized in Table 5, for different time periods, before CO 2 curing and compression testing. 2.2.3. Steam curing of specimens Steam curing is widely used for curing of concrete blocks. The steam curing scheme used in this study was: 2 h of moist pre-conditioning, 4 h of temperature elevation from room temperature to 70 /C176C, 10 h of constant temperature at 70 /C176C, then 2 h of cooling to room temperature. Compressive strength was measured immediately after completion of the steam curing cycle. 2.2.4. CO 2 curing of specimens The CO 2 curing set-up is illustrated in Fig. 2 . The pressure inside the curing chamber was monitored by a pressure gauge attached on the curing chamber. After the designed pre-conditioning period, the specimens were put into the curing chamber for CO 2 curing. Then the curing chamber with specimens was closed, vac- uumed to a pressure of around 600 mm Hg and maintained for 2 min before CO 2 was injected. The CO 2 pressures used for curing were 10 and 20 Psi. The pressure of CO 2 inside the chamber was controlled by a regulator on the CO 2 tank and kept constant during the CO 2 curing period. Different pre-conditioning conditions and curing time periods were also used to see how they affected the effectiveness of CO2 curing. Table 6 Strengths after steam curing. Effect of pre-conditioning times and conditions, and carbonation on strength of specimens. C. Shi et al. / Construction and Building Materials 26 (2012) 257–267 259

2.2.5. Temperature measurement during CO 2 curing The CO2 curing of unhydrated Portland cements is an exothermal reaction pro- cess. In this study, one thermal couple was embedded in the center of a specimen, one attached on the surface and one suspended in the chamber, to measure the temperature changes during the CO 2 curing in order to understand temperature change during the CO 2 curing process. 2.2.6. Moisture loss and CO 2 consumption The moisture loss during the pre-conditioning and CO 2 curing was measured. Based on the moisture loss and the masses of specimen before and after CO 2 curing, the CO2 consumption by cement in the specimen during CO 2 curing is calculated by the following equation: MCO2 ð%Þ¼ M2 /C0 M1 þ Mw C /C2 100 ð1Þ where MCO2 is the mass percentage of CO 2 consumed by cement,%, M1 is mass of the specimen before CO 2 curing, g, M2 is mass of the specimen after CO 2 curing, g, Mw is mass of measured moisture evaporation from a specimen during the CO 2 curing, g, and C is the mass of cement in the specimen, g Evaporated moisture was calculated by measuring the mass increase of the CO 2 curing chamber before and after CO 2 curing. 2.2.7. Strength measurement After CO 2 curing, the strength of cured specimens was measured immediately. Due to the specimen length to diameter ratio being one, a correction factor of 0.87 was applied for all directly measured compressive strength according to ASTM C39 [23]. 2.2.8. Examination of reaction by spraying phenolphthalein solution After testing in compression and/or split tension, a phenolphthalein solution containing 1% phenolphthalein in 70% ethyl alcohol was sprayed onto the fractured surface to measure the reacted depth. The solution turns into bright-red immedi- ately once it is sprayed onto the surface of non-carbonated concrete due to its high pH, while it is still colorless when it is sprayed onto reacted concrete due to the de- crease of pH after the reactions between CO 2 and clinker minerals and hydration products. Effect of moisture loss during the pre-conditioning period on compressive strength and CO 2 consumption after CO 2 curing. 260 C. Shi et al. / Construction and Building Materials 26 (2012) 257–267

3. Experimental results 3.1. Strength after steam curing The compressive and split tensile strengths of specimens after steam curing are listed in Table 6. Split tensile strength of Mixtures B3 and B4 were not measured due to limited number of specimens. Mixtures A2, A3, A4 and B2 met the minimum compressive strength requirement for load-bearing blocks. Mixture A series had a lower volume ratio of LWA to ﬁne sand than mixture B series. Regardless of the strength value, all these batches showed almost the same compressive/split tensile strength ratio. Mixtures with high LWA content allowed CO 2 to more easily penetrate into the i specimens due to its porous nature. Therefore, Mixture B 2 was se- lected for the laboratory CO 2 curing study in this project. 3.2. Effect of pre-conditioning time on strength and CO 2 consumption Fig. 3 a shows the effect of pre-conditioning time on compres- sive strength of specimens after moist pre-conditioning and 2 h of CO2 curing. After a total of 4–6 h of curing, the specimens with- out CO2 curing exhibited very low strength, which was lower than one MPa. As the total curing time was increased to 6 h, the strength of these specimens increased very signiﬁcantly. The specimens after CO2 curing showed much higher strength than these without CO2 curing. The strength of the specimens after CO 2 curing in- creased as the pre-conditioning time increased, especially with 18 h of pre-conditioning time. Actually, the specimens after 18 h of pre-conditioning and 2 h of CO 2 curing exhibited strength only slightly lower than those through 2 h of pre-conditioning time and 16 h of steam curing. Fig. 3b shows the effect of pre-conditioning time and CO 2 curing on compressive strength of specimens after dry pre-conditioning and 2 h of CO 2 curing. The specimens pre-conditioned in dry envi- ronment for 2–18 h exhibited almost the same strength as those pre-conditioned in the moist environment. However, the specimen pre-conditioned in the dry environment for 18 h had slightly lower strength than those pre-conditioned in the moist environment. This difference might be attributed to the water evaporation of the specimens in dry environment, which decreased the hydration of cement and resulted in lower strength. One interesting phenomena is that all the specimens pre- conditioned in the dry environment showed almost the same strength after 2 h of CO 2 curing, which was only slightly lower than those cured in steam. This means that the total curing time could be reduced to 4 h with the CO 2 curing, compared with a total time period of 20–24 h (including temperature rise and drop periods) for steam curing, which may be of signiﬁcant economic beneﬁts in addition to these technical beneﬁts. Under moist pre-conditioning environment, only a small amount of moisture (0.51%) was lost during the pre-conditioning. The different pre-conditioning times did not show an obvious ef- fect on CO 2 consumption. Under dry pre-conditioning environ- ment, the average moisture was 4.13%. However, the specimens exhibited much higher CO 2 consumption than those pre- conditioned in the moist environment. Also, the CO 2 consumption decreased slightly as the pre-conditioning time increased. 3.3. Effect of moisture loss during the pre-conditioning period on compressive strength and CO 2 consumption It is well known that relative humidity has a great effect on the carbonation of hardened cement concrete. In this project, different pre-conditioning condi

the mass of the cement. This means that about 40–50% of the ce- ment in the samples reacted with CO 2 during the CO 2 curing. They were signiﬁcantly higher than those non-conditioned specimens, which demonstrated CO 2 consumptions of less than 14% by the mass of the cement. The CO 2 consumptions of non-conditioned specimens were very similar to the values reported for wet cement powder or highly compacted cement samples [15]. The temperature of concrete specimens reached almost 60 /C176C after being exposed to CO 2 for 10–15 min. This means that the CO2 curing process is an exothermal reaction process and the reactions happen very fast. CO 2 rapidly reacts with calcium sili- cate in the presence of water or water vapor. However, when most of mineral surface has reacted, a continuous layer of car- bonation products acts as a barrier to the two-way diffusion and hinders the reaction [6]. In order to mitigate the inhibiting effect of this passivating layer, different approaches have been suggested [10], including raised temperatures and pressures, grinding and the use of chemical additives, such as NaHCO 3, NaCl and acid. Since the reactions between CO 2 and cement minerals release a signiﬁcant amount of heat, elevated tempera- ture may not be a very efﬁcient route. 5. Conclusions Based on the results from this study, the following conclusions can be drawn: – The control of proper amount of moisture in the concrete spec- imens through a pre-conditioning period is critical to the CO 2 curing process. There is an optimum moisture content in the specimens for CO 2 curing, with which the specimens demon- strate the highest CO 2 consumption and compressive strength. – The length of pre-conditioning period is not important to the CO2 curing process. However, the rate of moisture evaporation of concrete specimens during the pre-conditioning period has to be controlled. Fast moisture loss may result in the cracking of the specimens. – The temperature of the specimens elevated very quickly once the specimens were cured in CO 2 environment. The highest temperature could reach 70 /C176C during ﬁrst 15–20 min, then decreased with time. Based on the temperature proﬁles, it could be concluded that the chemical reactions during CO 2 curing occur mainly within the ﬁrst 120 min. 15 20 25 30 35 40 45 50 55 60 65 70 75 Time (min) Temperature (C) 01-Inside 02-Surface 03-Chamber (c) 8 h of Moist Preconditioning (moisture loss = 0.2%) 15 20 25 30 35 40 45 50 55 60 65 70 75 0 1 02 03 04 05 06 07 08 09 0 1 0 0 1 1 0 1 2 0 0 1 02 03 04 05 06 07 08 09 0 1 0 0 1 1 0 1 2 0 Time (min) Temperature (C) 01-Inside 02-Surface 03-Chamber (d) 18 h of Moist Preconditioning (moisture loss = 0.48%) Fig. 7 (continued) C. Effect of pre-conditioning on CO 2 curing of lightweight concrete blocks mixtures Caijun Shi a,b,⇑, Fuqiang He c, Yanzhong Wu b a Key Laboratory of Building Safety and Energy Efﬁciency (Ministry of Education), College of Civil Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, People’ s Republic of China b CJS Technology USA Ltd., Williamsville, NY 14121, United States c Department of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Xiamen University of Technology, Xiamen 361024, People’s Republic of China article info Article history: Received 11 January 2011 Received in revised form 29 April 2011 Accepted 13 June 2011 Available online 8 July 2011 Keywords: Concrete curing CO 2 consumption Pre-conditioning Lightweight concrete Moisture evaporation abstract It is a common practice to use steam to cure precast concrete products and concrete masonry blocks after casting. Steam curing is an energy extensive process and contributes signiﬁcantly to the production costs. This study investigated the effects of pre-conditioning on the CO 2 curing of lightweight concrete block mixtures. The results indicated that there was an optimum moisture loss at which the specimens dem- onstrated the highest strength and CO 2 consumption after CO 2 curing. However, the moisture evapora- tion rate had to be controlled so to avoid plastic shrinkage cracking. CO 2 curing was an exothermal process, which raised the temperature of specimens very quickly within a short period of time. Tradi- tional steam curing of concrete blocks took 18–24 h, CO 2 curing could be completed with 4–8 h including pre-conditioning to achieve strength equivalent to that steam curing, This means that the use of CO 2 cur- ing technology has advantages not only in reducing and/or utilizing greenhouse gas emissions, but also in decreasing the curing time and increasing productivity of plain and non-steel reinforced concrete products. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Carbon dioxide (CO 2) is the dominant greenhouse gas resulting from human activities. The CO 2 discharged to the atmosphere comes mostly from centralized sources, such as thermal power plants, building and transportation. More than 40% of anthropo- genic CO2 emissions are attributed to the combustion of fossil fuels for the generation of electricity [1]. The Kyoto Protocol on combat- ing climate change was passed in 1997 by over 180 nations with the intent of reducing global carbon emissions to a 1990 baseline. However, emissions have continued to rise, as some countries have been unwilling to take the economic hits that reduced emissions are likely to require [2,3]. In 2009, more than 120 heads of government attended the Copenhagen Climate Change Summit. Both developed and devel- oping nations agreed for the ﬁrst time to reduce their emissions and to register their national commitments. US announced a target to reduce emissions in the range of 17% below 2005 levels by 2020, 42% below 2005 levels by 2030, and 83% below 2005 levels by 2050. By 2020, China has committed to reduce its CO 2 emissions per unit of GDP by 40–45% from 2005 levels and use non-fossil fuels for about 15% of its energy. China has also committed to in- crease forest cover by 40 million hectares and forest stock volume by 1.3 billion cubic meters by 2020 (from 2005 levels). Some devel- oped countries also committed to delivering ‘‘prompt start’’ fund- ing to assist developing countries in deploying clean energy technologies, reducing forest-related em

with in CO 2 with different CO 2 pressures and time periods. The results in these ﬁgures indicated that there is an optimum mois- ture loss around 4.5% for CO 2 curing. As the moisture loss in- creased, both the strength and CO 2 consumption increased. However, as the moisture loss was greater than the optimum value, the strength decreased sharply although the CO 2 con- sumption did not changed much. Fig. 5 shows the color of the fracture surface of specimens pre-conditioned under different conditions, then cured for 1 h in 20 Psi CO 2. A specimen pre-conditioned in the moist environ- ment had 0.6% moisture loss during the pre-conditioning period and exhibited minimum CO 2 curing degree, as demonstrated in Fig. 4 , and bright pink color after phenolphthalein spray, as shown in Fig. 5 a. When the specimen was pre-conditioned in a marginally drier environment and had a moisture loss of 2.3%, the specimen exhibited only slightly pink, as shown in Fig. 5 b. This means that higher degree of reactions happened be- tween CO 2 and cement minerals during the CO 2 curing. When the moisture loss was further increased, then the color of the fracture surface did not change at all, as shown in Fig. 5 c and d. This means that pH of the fracture surface of specimens be- came neutral after the CO 2 curing. The optimum moisture loss for CO 2 curing can be explained from the mechanism of CO 2 curing. Water plays a very important role in CO 2 curing because the dissolution of CO 2 to form carbonic acid is the ﬁrst step in the CO 2 curing process. However, excess water blocks the pores and the movement of CO 2 from outside to inside of the specimen. On the other hand, lack of water inside will lead to less dissolution of CO 2 and decrease the reactions. The temperature and relative humidity of the curing environ- ment also inﬂuence the rate of CO 2 curing. Low relative humidity coupled with the CO 2 curing at high temperature can cause water loss readily from the cement-based matrix, which thus has nega- tive effects on reactions between CO 2 and cement minerals. The optimum moisture content of cement-based matrix could be reached by adjusting condition of cement-based matrix and curing environment accordingly. 3.4. Effect of pre-conditioning time and conditions on temperature increase Fig. 6 shows the effect of pre-conditioning time on temperature proﬁles inside specimens, at the surface of specimens and inside the curing chamber during the CO 2 curing after dry pre-conditioning.
